analogy: Think of "Agriculture" through the chapter's own opening images: India is an agriculturally important country. Two-thirds of its population is engaged in agricultural activities. Agriculture is a primary activity, which produces most of the food that we consume. Besides food grains, it also produces raw material for various industries. The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: TYPES OF FARMING, 31 AGRICULTURE, 32 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II, CROPPING PATTERN. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Agriculture is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that india is an agriculturally important country.

The text explains that two-thirds of its population is engaged in agricultural activities.

The text explains that agriculture is a primary activity, which produces most of the food that we consume.

The text explains that besides food grains, it also produces raw material for various industries.

The text explains that can you name some industries based on agricultural raw material?

The text explains that moreover, some agricultural products like tea, coffee, spices, etc. are also exported.

The text explains that tYPES OF FARMING Agriculture is an age-old economic activity in our country.

The text explains that over these years, cultivation methods have changed significantly depending upon the characteristics of physical environment, technological know-how and socio-cultural practices.

The text explains that farming varies from subsistence to commercial type.

The text explains that at present, in different parts of India, the following farming systems are practised.

The text explains that primitive Subsistence Farming This type of farming is still practised in few pockets of India.

The text explains that primitive subsistence agriculture is practised on small patches of land with the help of primitive tools like hoe, dao and digging sticks, and family/ community labour.

The text explains that this type of farming depends upon monsoon, natural fertility of the soil and suitability of other environmental conditions to the crops grown.

The text explains that farmers clear a patch of land and produce cereals and other food crops to sustain their family.

The text explains that when the soil fertility decreases, the farmers shift and clear a fresh patch of land for cultivation.

The text explains that this type of shifting allows Nature to replenish the fertility of the soil through natural processes; land productivity in this type of agriculture is low as the farmer does not use fertilisers or other modern inputs.

The text explains that intensive Subsistence Farming This type of farming is practised in areas of high population pressure on land.

The text explains that though the ‘right of inheritance’ leading to the division of land among successive generations has rendered land-holding size uneconomical, the farmers continue to take maximum output from the limited land in the absence of alternative source of livelihood.

The text explains that thus, there is enormous pressure on agricultural land.

The text explains that since the production is mainly for market, a well- developed network of transport and communication connecting the plantation areas, processing industries and markets plays an important role in the development of plantations.

The text explains that it is a kharif crop which requires high temperature, (above 25°C) and high humidity with annual rainfall above 100 cm.

The text explains that this rabi crop requires a cool growing season and a bright sunshine at the time of ripening.

The text explains that it is a kharif crop which requires temperature between 21°C to 27°C and grows well in old alluvial soil.

The text explains that it is the main source of sugar, gur (jaggary), khandsari and molasses.

The text explains that these are the major source of protein in a vegetarian diet.

The text explains that it grows well in hot and humid climate with a temperature of 21°C to 27°C and an annual rainfall between 75cm. and 100cm.

The text explains that tea is processed within the tea garden to restore its freshness.

The text explains that 4.11: Tea-leaves Harvesting Source: Source: Source: Source: Source: Pocket book of agricultural statistics, 2022, Govt. of India.

The text explains that it requires moist and humid climate with rainfall of more than 200 cm. and temperature above 25°C.

The text explains that high temperature is required during the time of growth.

The text explains that inspite of development of sources of irrigation most of the farmers in large parts of the country still depend upon monsoon and natural fertility in order to carry on their agriculture.

The text explains that the right of inheritance had already led to fragmentation of land holdings necessitating consolidation of holdings.

The text explains that the Government of India embarked upon introducing agricultural reforms to improve Indian agriculture in the 1960s and 1970s.

The text explains that it requires high temperature, light rainfall or irrigation, 210 frost-free days and bright sun-shine for its growth.

The text explains that provision for crop insurance against drought, flood, cyclone, fire and disease, establishment of Grameen banks, cooperative societies and banks for providing loan facilities to the farmers at lower rates of interest were some important steps in this direction.

The text explains that kisan Credit Card (KCC), Personal Accident Insurance Scheme (PAIS) are some other schemes introduced by the Government of India for the benefit of the farmers.

The text explains that the government also announces minimum support price, remunerative and procurement prices for important crops to check the exploitation of farmers by speculators and middlemen.

The text explains that vinoba Bhave could not promise it to them immediately but assured them to talk to the Government of India regarding provision of land for them if they undertook cooperative farming.

The text explains that (i) Which one of the following describes a system of agriculture where a single crop is grown on a large area?

The text explains that (iii) Enlist the various institutional reform programmes introduced by the government in the interest of farmers.

The text explains that (i) Suggest the initiative taken by the government to ensure the increase in agricultural production.

The text explains that (ii) Describe the geographical conditions required for the growth of rice.

The text explains that group discussion on the necessity of literacy among farmers.

The text explains that on an outline map of India show wheat producing areas.

The text explains that 41 AGRICULTURE ACTIVITY Solve the puzzle by following your search horizontally and vertically to find the hidden answers.

The text explains that pulses like arhar, moong, gram, urad contain… 4 .

The text explains that the two important beverages in India are… 6 .

The text explains that one of the four major fibers grown on black soils.

prelims_traps: Do not treat TYPES OF FARMING and 31 AGRICULTURE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Agriculture; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use TYPES OF FARMING and 31 AGRICULTURE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
